#REDIRECT [[暦学]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:れきとう}} [[Category:暦|*れきとう]] [[Category:暦法|*れきとう]] [[Category:天文学]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]]

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて[[陰陽寮]]の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は[[暦注]]の記入と毎年の暦の頒布、[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）などが用いられた。
[[平安時代]]中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の家学となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は従来の宣明暦を固守するに留まる。代わって符天暦を用いた[[宿曜道]]の台頭に至り、暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[閏置法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に、[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
{{DEFAULTSORT:れきとう}} [[Category:暦|*れきとう]] [[Category:暦法|*れきとう]] [[Category:天文学]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]]

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて[[陰陽寮]]の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は[[暦注]]の記入と毎年の暦の頒布、[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）などが用いられた。
[[平安時代]]中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の家学となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は従来の宣明暦を固守するに留まる。代わって符天暦を用いた[[宿曜道]]の台頭に至り、暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[閏置法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に、[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて[[陰陽寮]]の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は毎年の[[暦注]]の記入と暦の頒布（[[具注暦]]）、[[七曜暦]]・[[中星暦]]の作成や[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）、『[[定天論]]』・『[[周髀算経]]』などが用いられた他、[[算道]]の教科書である『[[九章算術]]』・『[[六章]]』などの数学書も用いられている。
[[平安時代]]中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の家学となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は従来の宣明暦を固守するに留まる。代わって符天暦を用いた[[宿曜道]]の台頭に至り、暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[閏置法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に、[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
[[室町時代]]に入ると、暦博士が[[天文密奏]]を行う例も見られるが、賀茂氏の嫡流である[[勘解由小路家]]の断絶により、[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]が暦道の主導権を握り、賀茂氏の庶流[[幸徳井家]]を圧迫して傘下に加えた。更に全国の[[陰陽師]]・[[暦師]]を傘下に加えて暦の販売に関して[[冥加金]]を取り立てる権限を獲得した。だが、[[明治維新]]後に陰陽寮が廃止され、暦道に代わって[[東京天文台]]が暦に関する業務を行うようになる。

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて[[陰陽寮]]の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は毎年の[[暦注]]の記入と暦の頒布（[[具注暦]]）、[[七曜暦]]・[[中星暦]]の作成や[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）、『[[定天論]]』・『[[周髀算経]]』などが用いられた他、[[算道]]の教科書である『[[九章算術]]』・『[[六章]]』などの数学書も用いられている。また、暦注の記載に関しては『[[大唐陰陽書]]』などを参照したとされている。[[平安時代]]に入ると、暦日と密接な方角禁忌が貴族の間で尊重されて、それらを知るための暦注が含まれた具注暦への需要が高まった。このため、次第に暦道も科学的な暦学から[[陰陽道]]的な色を帯びたものに変質していくことになる。
平安時代中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の[[家学]]となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は従来の宣明暦を固守するに留まる。代わって符天暦を用いた[[宿曜道]]の台頭に至り、暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために符天暦の仕組を巧に用いて「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[閏置法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
[[室町時代]]に入ると、暦博士が[[天文密奏]]を行う例も見られるが、賀茂氏の嫡流である[[勘解由小路家]]の断絶により、[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]が暦道の主導権を握り、賀茂氏の庶流[[幸徳井家]]を圧迫して傘下に加えた。更に全国の[[陰陽師]]・[[暦師]]を傘下に加えて暦の販売に関して[[冥加金]]を取り立てる権限を獲得した。だが、[[明治維新]]後に陰陽寮が廃止され、暦道に代わって[[東京天文台]]が暦に関する業務を行うようになる。

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて[[陰陽寮]]の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は毎年の[[暦注]]の記入と暦の頒布（[[具注暦]]）、[[七曜暦]]・[[中星暦]]の作成や[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）、『[[定天論]]』・『[[周髀算経]]』などが用いられた他、[[算道]]の教科書である『[[九章算術]]』・『[[六章]]』などの数学書も用いられている。また、暦注の記載に関しては『[[大唐陰陽書]]』などを参照したとされている。[[平安時代]]に入ると、暦日と密接な方角禁忌が貴族の間で尊重されて、それらを知るための暦注が含まれた具注暦への需要が高まった。このため、次第に暦道も科学的な暦学から[[陰陽道]]的な色を帯びたものに変質していくことになる。
平安時代中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の[[家学]]となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は従来の宣明暦を固守するに留まる。代わって符天暦を用いた[[宿曜道]]の台頭に至り、暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために符天暦の仕組を巧に用いて「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[閏置法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
[[室町時代]]に入ると、暦博士が[[天文密奏]]を行う例も見られるが、賀茂氏の嫡流である[[勘解由小路家]]の断絶により、[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]が暦道の主導権を握り、賀茂氏の庶流[[幸徳井家]]を圧迫して傘下に加えた。更に全国の[[陰陽師]]・[[暦師]]を傘下に加えて暦の販売に関して[[冥加金]]を取り立てる権限を獲得した。だが、[[明治維新]]後に陰陽寮が廃止され、暦道に代わって[[東京天文台]]が暦に関する業務を行うようになる。
{{DEFAULTSORT:れきとう}} [[Category:暦|*れきとう]] [[Category:暦法|*れきとう]] [[Category:日本の天文学]] [[Category:日本の教育の歴史]]

平安時代中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の[[家学]]となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は表面上は宣明暦を掲げながら、秘かに符天暦も併用した。その一方で、符天暦と[[密教]]が結びついた[[宿曜道]]の台頭が暦道を脅かした。更に当時の風習として、「[[朔旦冬至]]」（19年周期の最初の年に[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]が[[冬至]]となること。慶事とされる一方で、大規模な儀式に費用がかかった）の到来や回避、「19年周期に7回訪れる閏月の最初が閏8月ではいけない」、「大の月が4連続してはならない」などの迷信が信じられて、これに基づいて天体の動きとは無関係に人為的な暦の調整（「[[改暦]]」）が行われたほか、場合によっては暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために至り、符天暦の仕組を巧に用いて「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[閏置法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に[[鎌倉時代]]以後には[[仮名暦]]・[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
[[室町時代]]に入ると、暦博士が[[天文密奏]]を行う例も見られるが、賀茂氏の嫡流である[[勘解由小路家]]の断絶により、[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]が暦道の主導権を握り、賀茂氏の庶流[[幸徳井家]]を圧迫して傘下に加えた。更に全国の[[陰陽師]]・[[暦師]]を傘下に加えて暦の販売に関して[[冥加金]]を取り立てる権限を獲得した。だが、[[明治維新]]後に陰陽寮が廃止され、暦道に代わって[[東京天文台]]が暦に関する業務を行うようになる。
{{DEFAULTSORT:れきとう}} [[Category:暦|*れきとう]] [[Category:暦法|*れきとう]] [[Category:陰陽寮]] [[Category:日本の天文学]]

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて[[陰陽寮]]の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は毎年の[[暦注]]の記入と暦の頒布（[[具注暦]]）、[[七曜暦]]・[[中星暦]]の作成や[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）、『[[定天論]]』・『[[周髀算経]]』などが用いられた他、[[算道]]の教科書である『[[九章算術]]』・『[[六章]]』などの数学書も用いられている。また、暦注の記載に関しては『[[大唐陰陽書]]』などを参照したとされている。[[平安時代]]に入ると、暦日と密接な方角禁忌が貴族の間で尊重されて、それらを知るための暦注が含まれた具注暦への需要が高まった。このため、次第に暦道も科学的な暦学から[[陰陽道]]的な色を帯びたものに変質していくことになる。
平安時代中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の[[家学]]となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は表面上は宣明暦を掲げながら、秘かに符天暦も併用した。その一方で、符天暦と[[密教]]が結びついた[[宿曜道]]の台頭が暦道を脅かした。当初、暦道と宿曜道が共同して暦を作成していた時期もあった（『[[小右記]]』[[長和]]4年7月8日条）が、[[長暦]]2年（[[1038年]]）頃に両者の対立から宿曜道側が暦道の批判に回ったという（『[[春記]]』長暦2年11月27日条）。
更に当時の風習として、「[[朔旦冬至]]」（19年周期の最初の年に[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]が[[冬至]]となること。慶事とされる一方で、大規模な儀式に費用がかかった）の到来や回避、「19年周期に7回訪れる閏月の最初が閏8月ではいけない」、「大の月が4連続してはならない」などの迷信が信じられて、これに基づいて天体の動きとは無関係に人為的な暦の調整（「[[改暦]]」）が行われたほか、場合によっては暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために至り、符天暦の仕組を巧に用いて「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[置閏法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に[[鎌倉時代]]以後には[[仮名暦]]・[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
[[室町時代]]に入ると、暦博士が[[天文密奏]]を行う例も見られるが、賀茂氏の嫡流である[[勘解由小路家]]の断絶により、[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]が暦道の主導権を握り、賀茂氏の庶流[[幸徳井家]]を圧迫して傘下に加えた。更に全国の[[陰陽師]]・[[暦師]]を傘下に加えて暦の販売に関して[[冥加金]]を取り立てる権限を獲得した。だが、[[明治維新]]後に陰陽寮が廃止され、暦道に代わって[[東京天文台]]が暦に関する業務を行うようになる。
[[Category:暦|*れきとう]] [[Category:暦法|*れきとう]] [[Category:陰陽寮]] [[Category:日本の天文学史]]

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。[[陰陽寮]]で教えられていたものの一つ。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて陰陽寮の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は毎年の[[暦注]]の記入と暦の頒布（[[具注暦]]）、[[七曜暦]]・[[中星暦]]の作成や[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）、『[[定天論]]』・『[[周髀算経]]』などが用いられた他、[[算道]]の教科書である『[[九章算術]]』・『[[六章]]』などの数学書も用いられている。また、暦注の記載に関しては『[[大唐陰陽書]]』などを参照したとされている。[[平安時代]]に入ると、暦日と密接な方角禁忌が貴族の間で尊重されて、それらを知るための暦注が含まれた具注暦への需要が高まった。このため、次第に暦道も科学的な暦学から[[陰陽道]]的な色を帯びたものに変質していくことになる。
平安時代中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の[[家学]]となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は表面上は宣明暦を掲げながら、秘かに符天暦も併用した。その一方で、符天暦と[[密教]]が結びついた[[宿曜道]]の台頭が暦道を脅かした。当初、暦道と宿曜道が共同して暦を作成していた時期もあった（『[[小右記]]』[[長和]]4年7月8日条）が、[[長暦]]2年（[[1038年]]）頃に両者の対立から宿曜道側が暦道の批判に回ったという（『[[春記]]』長暦2年11月27日条）。
更に当時の風習として、「[[朔旦冬至]]」（19年周期の最初の年に[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]が[[冬至]]となること。慶事とされる一方で、大規模な儀式に費用がかかった）の到来や回避、「19年周期に7回訪れる閏月の最初が閏8月ではいけない」、「大の月が4連続してはならない」などの迷信が信じられて、これに基づいて天体の動きとは無関係に人為的な暦の調整（「[[改暦]]」）が行われたほか、場合によっては暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために改暦を行うに至り、符天暦の仕組を巧に用いて「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[置閏法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に[[鎌倉時代]]以後には[[仮名暦]]・[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
[[室町時代]]に入ると、暦博士が[[天文密奏]]を行う例も見られるが、賀茂氏の嫡流である[[勘解由小路家]]の断絶により、[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]が暦道の主導権を握り、賀茂氏の庶流[[幸徳井家]]を圧迫して傘下に加えた。更に全国の[[陰陽師]]・[[暦師]]を傘下に加えて暦の販売に関して[[冥加金]]を取り立てる権限を獲得した。だが、[[明治維新]]後に陰陽寮が廃止され、暦道に代わって[[東京天文台]]が暦に関する業務を行うようになる。
{{DEFAULTSORT:れきとう}} [[Category:暦|*れきとう]] [[Category:暦法|*れきとう]] [[Category:陰陽寮]] [[Category:日本の天文学史]]

'''暦道'''（れきどう）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における[[暦]]を作成するための[[学問]]（[[暦学]]）のことである。[[陰陽寮]]で教えられていたものの一つ。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に[[百済]]の暦博士が来日した記事が記されているが、本格的な暦学の伝来は[[推古天皇]]12年（[[602年]]）の百済[[僧]]・[[観勒]]の来日による。従って日本の暦道の起源はここに求められる。
[[律令制]]においては、[[大学寮]]ではなくて陰陽寮の管轄とされ、[[太陽]]・[[月]]を観測して暦を作成することが規定されて[[暦博士]]（1名もしくは2名）・[[暦生]]（10名）などが定められたが、実際には[[中国]]の[[暦法]]を輸入して日本で採用していたため、暦道は毎年の[[暦注]]の記入と暦の頒布（[[具注暦]]）、[[七曜暦]]・[[中星暦]]の作成や[[日食]]の予測に限定され、また[[天文道]]や[[算道]]とも被るところがあったため振るわなかった。このため、[[天平]]2年（[[730年]]）より暦生のうち優秀者2名を[[暦得業生]]として給費を行うなどの措置を取った。教科書としては『[[漢書]]』・『[[晋書]]』の暦律志や採用されている暦の注釈書（『[[大衍暦議]]』・『[[宣明暦経]]』）、『[[定天論]]』・『[[周髀算経]]』などが用いられた他、[[算道]]の教科書である『[[九章算術]]』・『[[六章]]』などの数学書も用いられている。また、暦注の記載に関しては『[[大唐陰陽書]]』などを参照したとされている。[[平安時代]]に入ると、暦日と密接な方角禁忌が貴族の間で尊重されて、それらを知るための暦注が含まれた具注暦への需要が高まった。このため、次第に暦道も科学的な暦学から[[陰陽道]]的な色を帯びたものに変質していくことになる。
平安時代中期の[[賀茂保憲]]が子の[[賀茂光栄|光栄]]に伝授して以後、[[賀茂氏]]の[[家学]]となるが、保憲は日食予想の成功率の低下などを憂慮して[[呉越]]に留学していた[[日延]]（[[天台宗]]）に依頼して持ち帰らせた[[符天暦]]の採用を図ったが朝廷に採用されず、光栄以後は表面上は宣明暦を掲げながら、秘かに符天暦も併用した。その一方で、符天暦と[[密教]]が結びついた[[宿曜道]]の台頭が暦道を脅かした。当初、暦道と宿曜道が共同して暦を作成していた時期もあった（『[[小右記]]』[[長和]]4年7月8日条）が、[[長暦]]2年（[[1038年]]）頃に両者の対立から宿曜道側が暦道の批判に回ったという（『[[春記]]』長暦2年11月27日条）。
更に当時の風習として、「[[朔旦冬至]]」（19年周期の最初の年に[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]が[[冬至]]となること。慶事とされる一方で、大規模な儀式に費用がかかった）の到来や回避、「19年周期に7回訪れる閏月の最初が閏8月ではいけない」、「大の月が4連続してはならない」などの迷信が信じられて、これに基づいて天体の動きとは無関係に人為的な暦の調整（「[[改暦]]」）が行われたほか、場合によっては暦算の誤りとそれを隠すために改暦を行うに至り、符天暦の仕組を巧に用いて「口伝・秘伝」と称して本来あり得ない[[置閏法]]の設定などによる暦を作成して極秘に調整する（日食や[[夏至]]・[[冬至]]の予定が狂う可能性が高くなる）などの暦道の振舞いに対して算道・宿曜道側からの激しい攻撃を受けた。更に[[鎌倉時代]]以後には[[仮名暦]]・[[民間暦]]も登場した事によりその地位は大きく低下する事となる。
[[室町時代]]に入ると、暦博士が[[天文密奏]]を行う例も見られるが、賀茂氏の嫡流である[[勘解由小路家 (賀茂氏)|勘解由小路家]]の断絶により、[[土御門家 (安倍氏)|土御門家]]が暦道の主導権を握り、賀茂氏の庶流[[幸徳井家]]を圧迫して傘下に加えた。更に全国の[[陰陽師]]・[[暦師]]を傘下に加えて暦の販売に関して[[冥加金]]を取り立てる権限を獲得した。だが、[[明治維新]]後に陰陽寮が廃止され、暦道に代わって[[東京天文台]]が暦に関する業務を行うようになる。
{{DEFAULTSORT:れきとう}} [[Category:暦|*れきとう]] [[Category:暦法|**れきとう]] [[Category:陰陽寮]] [[Category:日本の天文学史]]

The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje mon..."
revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Calendar Scholars (Rekihakase)|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
### Academic Materials and Evolution Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
### Kamo Clan and Later Developments The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
### Decline and Legacy During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.
[[ja:暦道]] [[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese culture]]

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Calendar Scholars (Rekihakase)|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Calendar Scholars (Rekihakase)|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Calendar Scholars (Rekihakase)|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.

Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.

Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.

Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.
[[ja:暦道]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Kk]][[en:Calendar-making]]

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Calendar Scholars (Rekihakase)|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.
[[ja:暦道]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Kk]][[en:Calendar-making]]

By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.
[[ja:暦道]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Calendar Science]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[en:Calendar-making]]

Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wu-Yue|ja|呉越}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming Calendar|ja|宣明暦}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the {{ill|Tokyo Astronomical Observatory|ja|東京天文台}}, marking the end of Rekidō.

Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.

Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
