[[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Calendar Science]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Calendar-making]]

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦|12=draft|12=draft}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft|12=draft}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|12=draft|12=draft}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=draft|12=draft}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=draft|12=draft}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|12=draft|12=draft}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|12=draft|12=draft}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft|12=draft}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=draft|12=draft}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft|12=draft}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|12=draft|12=draft}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=draft|12=draft}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=draft|12=draft}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|12=draft|12=draft}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=draft|12=draft}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=draft|12=draft}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

<!--Calendar Science--> {{draft categories| [[Category:Calendar Science]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]] }} [[ja:暦道]] [[sn:Rekidō]] [[en:Calendar-making]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Calendar Science}}

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦|12=draft|12=draft}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft|12=draft}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|12=draft|12=draft}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=draft|12=draft}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=draft|12=draft}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|12=draft|12=draft}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|12=draft|12=draft}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft|12=draft}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=draft|12=draft}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft|12=draft}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|12=draft|12=draft}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=draft|12=draft}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=draft|12=draft}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦|12=draft|12=draft}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft|12=draft}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|12=draft|12=draft}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=draft|12=draft}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=draft|12=draft}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|12=draft|12=draft}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|12=draft|12=draft}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft|12=draft}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=draft|12=draft}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft|12=draft}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|12=draft|12=draft}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=draft|12=draft}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=draft|12=draft}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
<!--Calendar Science-->

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|1=Daigaku-ryō|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
