It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|qq=draft|qq=}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|qq=draft|qq=}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|qq=draft|qq=}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|qq=draft|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|qq=draft|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|qq=draft|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|qq=draft|qq=}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|qq=draft|qq=}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō. {{draft categories| |qq=}}

revision.text:
#redirect[[:en:暦道]]
[[ja:暦道|1=Rekidō]] [[Category:automatic wikipedia redirects 暦]]

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|1=Daigaku-ryō|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=|12=draft}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Calendar Science|qq=}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Calendar Science]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]]
}}

Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|1=Daigaku-ryō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Calendar Science]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]]
}}

The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje|qq=}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kinmei|qq=}}. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|1=Daigaku-ryō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigaku-ryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigaku-ryō|qq=}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun|qq=}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moon|qq=}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eclipses|qq=}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Han|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Yasunori|qq=}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Longlì|qq=}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}
[[sn:Rekidō]] [[ja:暦道]] [[en:Calendar-making]] [[ar:Rekidō]]
<!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science-->

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|calendars|ja|暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/calendars|qq=}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje|qq=}} visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kinmei|qq=}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwan-rak|ja|観勒|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gwan-rak|qq=}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|ja|大学寮|1=Daigaku-ryō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigaku-ryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigaku-ryō|qq=}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|ja|太陽|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun|qq=}} and {{ill|Moon|ja|月|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moon|qq=}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=}}, and predicting {{ill|eclipses|ja|日食|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eclipses|qq=}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|ja|漢書|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Han|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|ja|九章算術|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|ja|賀茂保憲|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Yasunori|qq=}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai|qq=}} monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Longlì|ja|長暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Longlì|qq=}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.

It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

[[sn:Rekidō]] [[ja:暦道]] [[en:Calendar-making]] [[ar:Rekidō]]
<!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--> [[Category:Specific calendars]]

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

[[sn:Rekidō]] [[ja:暦道]] [[en:Calendar-making]] [[ar:Rekidō]]
<!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--> [[Category:Specific calendars]] [[Category:Calendars]] [[Category:陰陽寮]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Calendar Science]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Calendrical studies]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]]
}}

'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.

Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]]
[[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]]
}}
[[sn:Rekidō]] [[ja:暦道]]
[[ar:Rekidō]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11514958]] Wikidata name: rekidō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
