<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11514958]] Wikidata name: rekidō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|qq=|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

<!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--> [[Category:Specific calendars]] [[Category:Calendars]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]] {{wikidata link|Q11514958}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11514958]] Wikidata name: rekidō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. == Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11514958]] Wikidata name: rekidō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.

However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11514958]] Wikidata name: rekidō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[ar:Rekidō]]
<!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--> [[Category:Specific calendars]] [[Category:Calendars]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]] {{wikidata link|Q11514958}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

revision.comment: Bot: reorganize categories (alphabetical, deduplicated, moved to bottom)
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11514958]] Wikidata name: rekidō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of [[:en:Calendar|calendars]] (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]]. It was tasked with observing the [[:en:Sun|Sun]] and [[:en:Moon|Moon]] to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}

However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk [[:en:Gwalleuk|Gwan-rak]] in the 12th year of [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]] (602). However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting [[:en:Solar eclipse|eclipses]]. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). == Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''[[:en:Book of Han|Book of Han]]'' and ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''[[:en:The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art]]'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]]-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by [[:en:Kamo no Yasunori|Kamo no Yasunori]], passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk Nichien from [[:en:Wuyue|Wuyue]]. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the [[:en:Xuanming_calendar|Xuanming calendar]].
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by [[:en:Chōryaku|Longlì]] 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:暦道]]
[[ar:Rekidō]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11514958]] Wikidata name: rekidō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|WD=Q12132|lt=calendars}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwalleuk|en|Gwalleuk|WD=Q710949|lt=Gwan-rak}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|WD=Q525|lt=Sun}} and {{ill|Moon|en|Moon|WD=Q405|lt=Moon}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting {{ill|Solar eclipse|en|Solar eclipse|WD=Q3887|lt=eclipses}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|en|Book of Han|WD=Q846905|lt=Book of Han}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|en|Book of Jin|WD=Q1154753|lt=Book of Jin}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|en|The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|WD=Q657057|lt=Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|en|Kamo no Yasunori|WD=Q6358935|lt=Kamo no Yasunori}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wuyue|en|Wuyue|WD=Q1193822|lt=Wuyue}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming_calendar|en|Xuanming_calendar|WD=Q2511456|lt=Xuanming calendar}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Chōryaku|en|Chōryaku|WD=Q1193519|lt=Longlì}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:暦道]]
[[ar:Rekidō]]
<!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]] {{wikidata link|Q11514958}} [[Category:Calendars]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:Specific calendars]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]]

Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming_calendar|en|Xuanming_calendar|WD=Q2511456|lt=Xuanming calendar}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Chōryaku|en|Chōryaku|WD=Q1193519|lt=Longlì}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:暦道]]
[[ar:Rekidō]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]]
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|WD=Q12132|lt=calendars}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwalleuk|en|Gwalleuk|WD=Q710949|lt=Gwan-rak}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|WD=Q525|lt=Sun}} and {{ill|Moon|en|Moon|WD=Q405|lt=Moon}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting {{ill|Solar eclipse|en|Solar eclipse|WD=Q3887|lt=eclipses}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|en|Book of Han|WD=Q846905|lt=Book of Han}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|en|Book of Jin|WD=Q1154753|lt=Book of Jin}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|en|The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|WD=Q657057|lt=Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|en|Kamo no Yasunori|WD=Q6358935|lt=Kamo no Yasunori}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wuyue|en|Wuyue|WD=Q1193822|lt=Wuyue}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming_calendar|en|Xuanming_calendar|WD=Q2511456|lt=Xuanming calendar}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Chōryaku|en|Chōryaku|WD=Q1193519|lt=Longlì}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:暦道]]
[[ar:Rekidō]]
<!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--><!--Calendar Science--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]] {{wikidata link|Q11514958}} [[Category:Calendars]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:History of astronomy in Japan]] [[Category:Onmyōryō]] [[Category:Specific calendars]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Japanische Kalenderkunde]][[ja:暦道]]
'''Rekidō''' (暦道) refers to the ancient Japanese study of {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|WD=Q12132|lt=calendars|1=Calendar}} (''rekigaku''), which was one of the disciplines taught in the {{ill|Yin-Yang Bureau|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|fr|Bureau d'Onmyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' records an article about calendar scholars from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] visiting during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}. However, the full-scale introduction of calendar studies is attributed to the arrival of the Baekje monk {{ill|Gwalleuk|en|Gwalleuk|WD=Q710949|lt=Gwan-rak|1=Gwalleuk}} in the 12th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (602). Thus, the origins of Japanese calendrical studies can be traced to this event.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Ritsuryō}} system, Rekidō fell under the jurisdiction of the Onmyōryō rather than the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō|1=Daigaku-ryō}}. It was tasked with observing the {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|WD=Q525|lt=Sun|1=Sun}} and {{ill|Moon|en|Moon|WD=Q405|lt=Moon|1=Moon}} to create calendars. Positions such as {{ill|Reki Hakase|lt=Doctors of Calendar|ja|暦博士|1=Reki Hakase|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|13=User:Immanuelle/Reki Hakase|qq=}} (1–2 individuals) and {{ill|Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja|暦生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Students (Rekisei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (10 individuals) were established. However, since Japan adopted Chinese calendrical systems, the scope of Rekidō became limited to writing annotations (''rekichū'') for calendars, distributing {{ill|Guchūreki|lt=Guchūreki|lt=Annotated calendars|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annotated calendars|qq=}}, preparing {{ill|seven-day week calendars|ja|七曜暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seven-day week calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nakasei calendars|ja|中星暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakasei calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and predicting {{ill|Solar eclipse|en|Solar eclipse|WD=Q3887|lt=eclipses|1=Solar eclipse}}. Overlapping functions with disciplines such as {{ill|Tenmondō|ja|天文道|1=Tenmondō (astrology)|lt=Tenmondō|qq= (astrology)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmondō (astrology)|qq=}} and {{ill|Sandō (mathematics)|ja|算道|1=Sandō_(mathematics)|2=ja|3=算道|lt=Sandō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō (mathematics)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandō_(mathematics)|qq=}} hindered its development.
== Academic Materials and Evolution == Textbooks included the calendrical sections of the ''{{ill|Book of Han|en|Book of Han|WD=Q846905|lt=Book of Han|1=Book of Han}}'' and ''{{ill|Book of Jin|en|Book of Jin|WD=Q1154753|lt=Book of Jin|1=Book of Jin}}'', as well as commentaries on adopted calendars like the ''{{ill|Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja|大衍暦議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dayan Calendar Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Xuanming Calendar Text|ja|宣明暦経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xuanming Calendar Text|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. Additional materials included the ''{{ill|The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|en|The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|WD=Q657057|lt=Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art|1=The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art}}'' and ''{{ill|Six Chapters|ja|六章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Chapters|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', which were also used in Sandō.
By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, aristocrats began to emphasize taboos associated with directions and days, heightening the demand for annotated calendars. This period saw a shift from scientific calendrical studies to a more {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}-influenced practice.
== Kamo Clan and Later Developments == The {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, led by {{ill|Kamo no Yasunori|en|Kamo no Yasunori|WD=Q6358935|lt=Kamo no Yasunori|1=Kamo no Yasunori}}, passed down Rekidō within their family. Yasunori sought to adopt the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, brought back by the {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai|1=Tiantai}} monk Nichien from {{ill|Wuyue|en|Wuyue|WD=Q1193822|lt=Wuyue|1=Wuyue}}. Although it was not officially accepted, subsequent generations secretly used it alongside the {{ill|Xuanming_calendar|en|Xuanming_calendar|WD=Q2511456|lt=Xuanming calendar|1=Xuanming calendar}}.
The rise of the {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Shukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukuyōdō|qq=}}, which linked the Futen Calendar with esoteric Buddhism, posed a significant challenge to Rekidō. Collaborative efforts between the two disciplines eventually deteriorated, culminating in conflict by {{ill|Chōryaku|en|Chōryaku|WD=Q1193519|lt=Longlì|1=Chōryaku}} 2 (1038). Misguided superstitions and deliberate adjustments to calendars, such as avoiding certain lunar alignments, undermined Rekidō's credibility and subjected it to criticism from Sandō and Shukuyōdō scholars.
== Decline and Legacy == During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the advent of {{ill|Kana Calendars|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kana Calendars|qq=}} and unofficial calendars further eroded Rekidō's authority. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} overshadowed the Kamo clan, consolidating control over calendar production and taxation.
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the Onmyōryō was abolished, and calendrical duties were transferred to the [[National Astronomical Observatory of Japan]], marking the end of Rekidō.
{{translated page|ja|暦道|version=96148501|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:暦道]]
